name: Science Education 7–10: Developing Professional Skills and Understanding
week0Message: Have a look around at the first few tiles below — we'll get started next week!

learningOutcomes.title: Science Content & Laboratory Practice
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the concepts, substance and structure of the content and teaching strategies of the 7–10 science syllabus by incorporating practical activities into the laboratory so that students are actively engaged in learning.

learningOutcomes.title: Curriculum Sequencing & Pedagogy
learningOutcomes.description: Organise content into an effective learning and teaching sequence within the context of the 7–10 syllabus in logical, sequential and structured ways utilising appropriate pedagogy to address student learning outcomes.

learningOutcomes.title: Lesson Planning & Conceptual Development
learningOutcomes.description: Plan lesson sequences using knowledge of student learning, content and effective teaching strategies by recognising links in teaching sequences which will develop conceptual understanding and scientific skills and clearly articulate the learning goals.

learningOutcomes.title: Assessment Design & Program Evaluation
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate broad knowledge of strategies that can be used to evaluate teaching programs to improve student learning by planning and documenting an assessment task which includes a range of embedded strategies for assessing student achievement and appropriately mapping outcomes to these strategies.

learningOutcomes.title: Diversity & Responsive Teaching
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of teaching strategies, including literacy and numeracy development, that are responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.

learningOutcomes.title: Professional Learning & Community
learningOutcomes.description: Understand the rationale for continued professional learning, and relevant sources of professional learning including external professionals and community representatives, that can be used to improve personal development and student learning.

assessmentTasks.title: Design and Pedagogy — Practical Science Lessons
assessmentTasks.rationale: Secondary school science teachers need to recognise opportunities for practicals as often as possible. The syllabus mandates a minimum amount of practical work, which can always be exceeded. It is essential that teachers can provide instruction and practical opportunities such that students build their own understanding, working with what they already know and can do.
assessmentTasks.aim: Design a coherent practical science learning sequence of at least 8–10 practicals for a chosen Stage 4 or Stage 5 NSW Science Years 7–10 (2026) Knowledge and Understanding strand, and justify your pedagogical, syllabus, safety, and cohort decisions. The given cohort is a class of 30 students including 2 EAL/D students, 2 advanced, 3 behind in science understanding, and 5 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students with strong local community connections.

parts.title: Practical science learning sequence
parts.description: Construct a table of at least 8–10 practicals arranged in a logical learning progression for a chosen Stage 4 or Stage 5 Knowledge and Understanding strand.
requirements: Choose either Stage 4 (Year 7 & 8) or Stage 5 (Year 9 & 10) and any one Knowledge and Understanding strand from the NSW Science Years 7–10 syllabus (2026).

For each practical, include: chronological number; title; description (20 words maximum); NSW syllabus outcomes with specific outcome codes and content statements; prior knowledge and skills required; safety considerations for this practical and the given cohort; adjustments for the diverse learners in the class.
The sequence must show a logical learning progression from foundational to more complex practicals.

parts.title: Justification
parts.description: Citing relevant literature, justify the decisions made in Part A.
requirements: Address your pedagogical approach and how it is evident in the sequence.
Explain how the sequence supports the given cohort, including adjustments for diverse learners.
Address relevant syllabus elements including Working Scientifically outcomes, General Capabilities, and Cross Curriculum Priorities.
Justify prior knowledge and safety decisions.
APA 7th reference list required.

parts.title: Reflection
parts.description: Reflect on your learning journey for this task and identify one specific professional learning source.
requirements: Reflect on what was new to you and what you already knew well.

Identify one specific professional learning source — such as a NESA resource, a professional association (e.g. ASTA), an experienced teacher, or a community representative — that could deepen your science content knowledge or teaching in your chosen strand.
Briefly justify your choice and identify any changes to how you approach your own professional learning.

rubric.criterion: Holistic assessment of the practical learning sequence — syllabus accuracy and depth, logical progression, constructivist pedagogy and prior knowledge, safety and inclusion for the given cohort

HD.descriptor: Syllabus: Comprehensive, nuanced, accurate. Sequence: Expertly progressive; builds understanding from foundational to complex. Pedagogy: Constructivism deeply evident; prior knowledge insightfully identified; goals precise. Safety/inclusion: Proactive professional judgement; nuanced, specific adjustments for all cohort groups.

D.descriptor: Syllabus: Thorough and accurate. Sequence: Logical; builds understanding well. Pedagogy: Constructivism clearly evident; prior knowledge accurately identified; goals clear. Safety/inclusion: Consistently applied; accurate adjustments for the given cohort.

C.descriptor: Syllabus: Sound accuracy; minor gaps. Sequence: Mostly logical; some gaps. Pedagogy: Constructivism evident; prior knowledge noted; goals present. Safety/inclusion: Correctly applied; adjustments address most cohort needs.

P.descriptor: Syllabus: Adequate; some inaccuracies. Sequence: Adequate order; limited progression. Pedagogy: Limited evidence; prior knowledge partially identified. Safety/inclusion: Inconsistently applied; adjustments partial.

N.descriptor: Syllabus: Inaccurate or missing. Sequence: Illogical or absent. Pedagogy: Not evident; prior knowledge absent. Safety/inclusion: Inadequate or absent.

rubric.criterion: Cited justification — scholarly and syllabus sources accurately cited; argument justifies pedagogical decisions, syllabus choices, prior knowledge, safety decisions, and adjustments for the given cohort

HD.descriptor: Evidence comprehensive and precisely cited. Justification demonstrates deep, integrated reasoning across all required elements: pedagogy, syllabus, safety, and cohort.

D.descriptor: Evidence thorough and well cited. Justification demonstrates strong reasoning across pedagogy, syllabus, safety, and cohort needs.

C.descriptor: Evidence sound and mostly cited. Justification addresses all required elements with sound reasoning.

P.descriptor: Evidence present but limited. Justification addresses some elements adequately.

N.descriptor: Evidence inadequate or absent. Justification does not address required elements.

rubric.criterion: Professional reflection and learning — reflects on new and prior knowledge from this task; names and justifies a specific professional learning source relevant to their teaching context and chosen strand

HD.descriptor: All prompt elements addressed with specificity and genuine professional reasoning. Professional learning source well-chosen and convincingly justified in relation to teaching context.

D.descriptor: All prompt elements addressed clearly. Source named and justified; connection to teaching context evident if not fully developed.

C.descriptor: Most prompt elements addressed. Source identified; justification limited or generic.

P.descriptor: Prompt elements addressed superficially. Source absent, unnamed, or unjustified.

N.descriptor: Not completed to an adequate standard or too generic to assess.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD work demonstrates comprehensive, nuanced syllabus accuracy across all practicals, expertly progressive sequencing, deeply evident constructivism with insightful prior knowledge identification, and proactive professional safety and inclusion judgement with specific adjustments for all cohort groups. Justification is precisely cited and integrates reasoning across all required elements. Reflection names a specific, well-chosen professional learning source convincingly justified in relation to teaching context.
checklist: Stage (4 or 5) and Knowledge and Understanding strand clearly named
At least 8–10 practicals in the table
Each practical includes: number, title, description (≤20 words), NSW outcomes and content codes, prior knowledge, safety considerations, cohort adjustments
Practicals arranged in a logical learning progression (foundational to complex)
Safety considerations are contemporary and specific to each practical
Adjustments address all named cohort groups: EAL/D, advanced, behind in science, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander students
Part B cites peer-reviewed literature accurately in APA 7th
Part C names a specific professional learning source and justifies the choice
Submitted on the template with APA 7th reference list
submissionInstructions: Submit on the template provided via the Moodle assignment link.
APA 7th reference list required. In-text citations, reference list, and syllabus extracts are excluded from the word count.
Submissions exceeding the word count by more than 10% may be penalised.

assessmentTasks.title: Unit Program — Constructivist Science Teaching
assessmentTasks.rationale: Unit programming is a core professional responsibility of secondary science teachers. Effective programs make a constructivist pedagogical approach visible across five weeks, integrate practical work, embed literacy, numeracy, and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives authentically, and include formative and summative assessment that gives all learners equitable access to demonstrate achievement.
assessmentTasks.aim: Develop a five-week unit program for a chosen Knowledge and Understanding strand using a named constructivist pedagogical approach, for the given cohort. Design a summative assessment task with an A–E marking rubric aligned to unit outcomes, and reflect on your learning and professional development needs. The given cohort is a class of 30 students including 2 EAL/D students, 2 advanced, 3 behind in science understanding, and 5 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students with strong local community connections.

parts.title: Unit program
parts.description: Develop a five-week unit program for the given cohort using a named constructivist pedagogical approach. Assume four 50-minute lessons per week, two of which are laboratory lessons (20 lessons total).
requirements: Clearly name your constructivist pedagogical approach — its key features must be recognisable throughout the program.
Include syllabus outcomes, content, and Working Scientifically skills across the five weeks.
Embed General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities.
Include teaching strategies that engage the diverse cohort, with adjustments for each learner group.
Include at least two formative assessment opportunities designed to inform future teaching.
Include at least one practical per week conducted in a laboratory lesson.
parts.note: Do NOT write 20 lesson plans. This is a high-level unit program. Word count excludes: in-text citations, reference list, syllabus extracts, and the unit program overview section (year group, strand, pedagogical approach, and rationale — typically before the weekly tables).

parts.title: Highlight and embed
parts.description: Return to your Part A unit program and apply three sets of highlights reflecting genuine embedding.
requirements: Blue highlight — literacy and numeracy strategies.
Yellow highlight — Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, histories, and cultures.
Green highlight — formative assessment tasks.
At least two formative assessment tasks must be present and highlighted green.
Highlights must reflect genuine embedding, not retrospective addition.

Note (LO4): The green formative assessment highlight is assessed separately under LO4 and carries its own criterion in the marking rubric. Each highlighted formative task should be purposefully connected to a learning goal and designed to inform future teaching decisions for the given cohort — not simply any activity that checks understanding.

parts.title: Summative assessment task with marking rubric
parts.description: Design a summative assessment task that captures student achievement at the end of your unit, with an A–E marking rubric.
requirements: The task may be individual or collaborative, theory or practical.
Must be completed in class — not a take-home or extended task.
Must be inclusive for all learners — consider adjustments, access arrangements, and alternative response modes.
Must align to syllabus outcomes addressed in Parts A and B.
Must include an A–E marking rubric using language from the NESA Science Years 7–10 Performance Band Descriptors.

parts.title: Reflection
parts.description: Reflect on your learning across this task and identify one aspect for program evaluation and one external professional or community source.
requirements: Reflect on what was most and least familiar across unit programming, assessment design, and planning for diverse learners.
Identify one aspect of your unit program you would evaluate or refine after teaching it, and describe what evidence you would use.

Name one external professional or community source — such as an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander community representative, a NESA consultant, or a science education professional association — whose input could improve your content knowledge or teaching.
Explain briefly how you would access that support.

rubric.criterion: Syllabus content, outcomes, skills, and practicals — accuracy and depth of syllabus outcomes, content, Working Scientifically skills mapped across the unit; practicals purposefully integrated at least once per week in lab lessons

HD.descriptor: Nuanced, comprehensive syllabus outcomes, content, and skills across all five weeks. Practicals expertly integrated and purposefully matched to content. Exceptional science content knowledge evident.

D.descriptor: Accurate syllabus outcomes, content, and skills throughout. Practicals well-integrated and matched to content. Strong science content knowledge evident.

C.descriptor: Mostly accurate syllabus elements across the unit. Practicals present and appropriate. Sound science content knowledge.

P.descriptor: Some accurate syllabus elements. Practicals present with some gaps or mismatches. Adequate content knowledge.

N.descriptor: Syllabus elements inaccurate or missing. Practicals absent, inadequate, or poorly matched. Content knowledge not adequately demonstrated.

rubric.criterion: Teaching sequence, strategies, and adjustments for the given cohort — constructivist approach named and evident; teaching strategies engage the diverse cohort; sequence builds understanding progressively; adjustments, General Capabilities, and Cross Curriculum Priorities embedded

HD.descriptor: Constructivist approach expertly named and sustained throughout. Innovative, cohort-responsive strategies. Sequence builds understanding with clear, expert progression. Nuanced adjustments and purposeful embedding of General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities.

D.descriptor: Constructivist approach clearly named and well-applied. Effective, cohort-responsive strategies. Sequence builds understanding well. Accurate adjustments and embedding of General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities.

C.descriptor: Constructivist approach named and evident. Appropriate strategies. Sequence mostly progressive. Sound adjustments; General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities present.

P.descriptor: Constructivist approach referenced but partially evident. Some strategies appropriate. Adequate progression. Limited adjustments; General Capabilities or Cross Curriculum Priorities partially addressed.

N.descriptor: Constructivist approach not named or evident. Strategies limited or inappropriate. Sequence does not build understanding. Adjustments absent or inadequate.

rubric.criterion: Literacy, numeracy, and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander embedding — blue (literacy/numeracy) and yellow (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander) highlights reflect genuine, effective embedding throughout the unit

HD.descriptor: Exceptional range genuinely and effectively embedded. All elements highlighted throughout.

D.descriptor: Strong range effectively embedded. All elements highlighted.

C.descriptor: Sound embedding with appropriate strategies and perspectives. Elements highlighted.

P.descriptor: Limited strategies embedded. Most elements highlighted.

rubric.criterion: Inclusion and cohort appropriateness — unit gives all learners in the given cohort equitable access; barriers to participation identified and mitigated

HD.descriptor: Barriers expertly identified and mitigated. Unit provides genuinely equitable access for all cohort groups.

D.descriptor: Barriers identified and mitigated. Unit provides clear access for all diverse learners.

C.descriptor: Some barriers identified. Unit is mostly accessible for diverse learners.

P.descriptor: Limited attention to barriers. Some adjustments present.

N.descriptor: Barriers not identified. Unit does not address diverse learner needs.

rubric.criterion: Formative assessment tasks — at least two tasks present and highlighted green; purposefully designed to generate evidence that informs subsequent teaching for the given cohort

HD.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Purposefully designed with explicit connection to learning goals and clear link to informing future teaching for the cohort.

D.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Well-chosen; connection to future teaching clearly evident.

C.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Appropriate; some indication of how they inform teaching.

P.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Generic or loosely connected to learning goals.

N.descriptor: Fewer than two tasks present or highlighted, or tasks would not inform teaching.

rubric.criterion: Task design and syllabus alignment — task details accurate and appropriate for cohort; clearly aligned to unit syllabus outcomes; student-facing instructions unambiguous and appropriate for in-class completion

HD.descriptor: Task details accurate and clearly appropriate. Exceptional alignment to syllabus outcomes. Instructions unambiguous, precise, and inclusive.

D.descriptor: Task details accurate and appropriate. Strong syllabus alignment. Instructions clear and concise.

C.descriptor: Task details accurate. Sound alignment. Instructions mostly clear.

P.descriptor: Task details mostly accurate. Adequate alignment to some outcomes. Instructions present with room for improvement.

N.descriptor: Errors in task details. Inadequate alignment. Instructions ambiguous or absent.

rubric.criterion: Internal alignment and appropriateness — task aligns to the unit program in Parts A and B; design gives all cohort members equitable opportunity to demonstrate achievement; inclusive for all learners

HD.descriptor: Task aligns closely and purposefully to Parts A and B. All cohort members have a genuine, equitable opportunity to demonstrate achievement.

D.descriptor: Task aligns well. Cohort members have a clear opportunity to demonstrate achievement.

C.descriptor: Task aligns to Parts A and B. Most cohort members can demonstrate achievement; access for some may be limited.

P.descriptor: Task aligns adequately. May not fully reflect outcomes or provide equitable access for all learners.

N.descriptor: Task does not align to Parts A and B and/or does not provide adequate opportunity for all learners.

rubric.criterion: Marking rubric quality — A–E rubric uses NESA Science Years 7–10 Performance Band Descriptor language; supports consistent teacher judgement and clear student understanding of expectations

HD.descriptor: Rubric strongly supports consistent marking and student understanding. Performance Band Descriptor language used very effectively across all five bands.

D.descriptor: Rubric supports consistent marking and student understanding. Performance Band Descriptor language used well.

C.descriptor: Rubric provides some support for consistent marking. Performance Band Descriptor language used soundly.

P.descriptor: Rubric present but may be poorly constructed. Performance Band Descriptor language attempted.

N.descriptor: Rubric absent, very poorly constructed, or Performance Band Descriptor language not used.

rubric.criterion: Professional reflection and learning — reflects on the learning journey; identifies an aspect for program evaluation with evidence; names and justifies an external professional or community source

HD.descriptor: Exceptional: specific evaluation aspect identified with precise evidence articulated. External source well-chosen and convincingly justified in relation to content knowledge or teaching of this unit.

D.descriptor: Critical reflection: evaluation aspect identified with evidence noted. External source named and justified; connection to practice evident.

C.descriptor: Some critical reflection: evaluation aspect noted. External source identified; justification present but limited.

P.descriptor: Surface reflection: evaluation aspect or external source absent or too vague to assess.

N.descriptor: Not completed to an adequate standard.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD work names a constructivist pedagogical approach and sustains it visibly across all five weeks, with expertly integrated practicals, nuanced cohort adjustments, and purposeful embedding of literacy, numeracy, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, and formative assessment. The summative task is closely aligned to the unit program, inclusive for all cohort members, and accompanied by a rubric that uses Performance Band Descriptor language effectively. Reflection identifies a specific evaluation aspect with precise evidence and a well-chosen external source convincingly justified.
checklist: Constructivist approach named explicitly — its features are visible throughout the program
Five weeks, four lessons per week (two lab lessons), 20 lessons total
At least one practical per week in a laboratory lesson
At least two formative assessment opportunities included
Syllabus outcomes, content, Working Scientifically skills, GCs and CCPs included
Adjustments included for all cohort groups: EAL/D, advanced, behind in science, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander students
Part B highlights: blue (literacy/numeracy), yellow (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander), green (formative assessment) — genuine, not retrospective
Part C summative task is in-class, inclusive, and aligned to unit outcomes
Part C rubric uses NESA Performance Band Descriptor language across all five bands (A–E)
Part D names a specific external professional or community source with a concrete access plan
Submitted on the template with APA 7th reference list
submissionInstructions: Submit on the template provided via the Moodle assignment link.
APA 7th reference list required. In-text citations, reference list, syllabus extracts, and the unit program overview section are excluded from the word count.
Do not write 20 lesson plans — this is a high-level unit program.
Submissions exceeding the word count by more than 10% may be penalised.

supportCallout: Anything going on that may impact your success in this unit? Don't hesitate to reach out to your lecturer sooner rather than later. We're here to support you.

1.title: The K–10 Continuum and Science Education

topics.title: The K–10 Continuum and Science Education
The Nature of Science and Science Teaching

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE362. This week we begin by stepping back from Years 7–10 and examining the full K–10 science continuum — the foundation of prior knowledge and experience your students bring with them into Years 7–10.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 1 examines how the NSW Science K–10 curriculum connects to the Years 7–10 syllabuses. You will trace the progression of key concepts, skills, and Working Scientifically strands from primary through to early secondary science, and consider how understanding this continuum helps you plan more responsive and connected Years 7–10 lessons.

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE362. This week we begin by stepping back from Years 7–10 and examining the full K–10 science continuum — the foundation of prior knowledge and experience your students bring with them into Years 7–10.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 1 examines how the NSW Science K–10 curriculum connects to the Years 7–10 syllabuses. You will trace the progression of key concepts, skills, and Working Scientifically strands from primary through to early secondary science, and consider how understanding this continuum helps you plan more responsive and connected Years 7–10 lessons.

1.liveSessionFocus: In our first live session we introduce the unit and cover Topics 1 and 2 together — examining the K–10 learning continuum and the Nature of Science as connected foundations for planning responsive and conceptually honest Years 7–10 lessons.
liveSessionTasks: Before the session, review the Science K–10 syllabus outcomes for Stage 5 in your primary teaching area and identify two or three concepts that directly underpin Years 7–10 content.
Consider: what assumptions do NSW Science Years 7–10 syllabus make about what students already know from K–10? Where might those assumptions break down?
Come prepared to share one example of a misconception or gap in prior knowledge you have observed (or anticipate) in senior science students.

2.title: Selecting and Critically Evaluating Resources

announcementBody.intro: This week we develop your skills as a critical consumer and selector of teaching resources — a practical and ongoing professional responsibility that shapes the quality of every lesson you teach.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 3 examines how to evaluate resources systematically and critically across different formats: textbooks, digital tools, laboratory kits, online platforms, and teacher-created materials. You will develop a personal evaluation framework grounded in syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and pedagogical appropriateness, and begin building a curated resource library for your teaching area.

announcementBody.intro: This week we develop your skills as a critical consumer and selector of teaching resources — a practical and ongoing professional responsibility that shapes the quality of every lesson you teach.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 3 examines how to evaluate resources systematically and critically across different formats: textbooks, digital tools, laboratory kits, online platforms, and teacher-created materials. You will develop a personal evaluation framework grounded in syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and pedagogical appropriateness, and begin building a curated resource library for your teaching area.

2.liveSessionFocus: Session 2 focuses on resource evaluation frameworks and strategies for building a coherent, high-quality teaching toolkit for Years 7–10 science.
liveSessionTasks: Select one textbook or digital resource used in your primary teaching area and evaluate it against your own criteria for quality — consider syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and how it supports student thinking.
Identify one specific strength and one clear weakness in the resource, and consider what you would do to address the weakness in practice.
Come prepared to share your evaluation briefly — we will compare criteria and approaches across different teaching areas in the session.

3.title: Introduction to Teaching Differentiation

topics.title: Introduction to Teaching Differentiation

announcementBody.intro: This week we focus on questioning as a teaching tool — specifically how the questions you ask (and how you ask them) shape the quality of thinking in your science classroom.
announcementBody.focus: We cover open and closed questions, levels of questioning from Bloom's Taxonomy, the Q-Matrix, Harvard Project Zero thinking routines, and the role of psychological safety in creating a classroom where students are willing to respond.

topics.orientationNote: The questioning techniques in this topic are directly transferable to your AT1 lesson sequence — the questions you write into your lesson plans are evidence of your ability to target specific levels of student thinking (LO1, AITSL 2.1.1). As you work through Bloom's and the Q-Matrix this week, think about where in your planned sequence you would place LOTS versus HOTS questions, and where a Harvard Project Zero thinking routine could replace a teacher-directed question entirely.
forumPrompts: Choose a NSW 7–10 science syllabus outcome for any Stage 4 or 5 topic. Write one closed question and one open question that target that outcome. Then write a third question using a stem from the Q-Matrix that moves students to a higher cognitive level. Post your three questions and briefly explain what mental operation each one requires. What does the difference in student response tell you as a teacher?

Read the Tofade et al. (2013) material on psychological safety or review the principles on the slide. Describe one specific teacher behaviour — from the list or from your own observation — that would undermine a student's willingness to answer a question in a science class. How would you avoid or correct that behaviour in your own practice? Connect your answer to the NSW Quality Teaching Framework's Quality Learning Environment dimension.

announcementBody.intro: This week we focus on questioning as a teaching tool — specifically how the questions you ask (and how you ask them) shape the quality of thinking in your science classroom.
announcementBody.focus: We cover open and closed questions, levels of questioning from Bloom's Taxonomy, the Q-Matrix, Harvard Project Zero thinking routines, and the role of psychological safety in creating a classroom where students are willing to respond.

3.liveSessionFocus: This session we will work through questioning frameworks hands-on — classifying question types, practising question stems from the Q-Matrix, and examining what makes a question genuinely open rather than a 'guess what I'm thinking' prompt.
liveSessionTasks: Questioning is a core pedagogical skill for Stage 4 and 5 science — the transition from Stage 3 Science and Technology, where students are often told answers, to Stage 4 Science, where they are expected to investigate and reason, depends heavily on the quality of questions teachers ask. As you work through Bloom's Taxonomy and the Q-Matrix this week, think about how you would sequence questions across a Stage 4 lesson: what closed question establishes prior knowledge, what open question surfaces a misconception, and what higher-order question asks students to evaluate evidence or predict an outcome. This skill of deliberate question design sits at the heart of LO1 and will recur throughout your AT1 planning work.
3.orientationNote: The questioning techniques in this topic are directly transferable to your AT1 lesson sequence — the questions you write into your lesson plans are evidence of your ability to target specific levels of student thinking (LO1, AITSL 2.1.1). As you work through Bloom's and the Q-Matrix this week, think about where in your planned sequence you would place LOTS versus HOTS questions, and where a Harvard Project Zero thinking routine could replace a teacher-directed question entirely.
forumPrompts: Choose a NSW 7–10 science syllabus outcome for any Stage 4 or 5 topic. Write one closed question and one open question that target that outcome. Then write a third question using a stem from the Q-Matrix that moves students to a higher cognitive level. Post your three questions and briefly explain what mental operation each one requires. What does the difference in student response tell you as a teacher?

Read the Tofade et al. (2013) material on psychological safety or review the principles on the slide. Describe one specific teacher behaviour — from the list or from your own observation — that would undermine a student's willingness to answer a question in a science class. How would you avoid or correct that behaviour in your own practice? Connect your answer to the NSW Quality Teaching Framework's Quality Learning Environment dimension.

4.title: The Inclusive Science Classroom

topics.title: The Inclusive Science Classroom

announcementBody.intro: Building on Topic 4, this week we move from the theory of differentiation into the practical challenge of creating genuinely inclusive Years 7–10 science classrooms — environments where every student can access, engage with, and be challenged by the curriculum.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 5 examines inclusive teaching in the Years 7–10 science context, with a focus on students with disability, students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds, and students at both ends of the achievement spectrum. You will explore specific adjustments, design principles, and communication strategies, and consider how to maintain academic rigour while broadening access.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 5 moves from the principles of differentiation explored in Topic 4 into the practical challenge of designing genuinely inclusive Years 7–10 science classrooms. The adjustment strategies, UDL principles, and equity frameworks you encounter this week are directly applicable to your work with diverse 7–10 science cohorts — and they also underpin the assessment design and unit planning you will engage with in Topic 6. As you work through the Topic 5 materials, pay attention to the reasoning behind specific adjustments: understanding why a particular strategy maintains access to the curriculum while preserving the integrity of the learning outcome will prepare you to make similar decisions in your own planning.

announcementBody.intro: Building on Topic 4, this week we move from the theory of differentiation into the practical challenge of creating genuinely inclusive Years 7–10 science classrooms — environments where every student can access, engage with, and be challenged by the curriculum.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 5 examines inclusive teaching in the Years 7–10 science context, with a focus on students with disability, students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds, and students at both ends of the achievement spectrum. You will explore specific adjustments, design principles, and communication strategies, and consider how to maintain academic rigour while broadening access.

4.liveSessionFocus: Session 4 focuses on the practical design of inclusive learning experiences in Years 7–10 science, including how to adapt tasks, resources, and assessment approaches without reducing the challenge of the curriculum.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one adjustment or scaffolding strategy from the topic materials that you could apply immediately to a Years 7–10 science lesson you are planning or have taught.
Consider how Years 7–10 syllabus requirements interact with NESA access arrangements — where do they align well, and where do tensions arise?
Come prepared to discuss a specific scenario: a student with a particular learning need in your Years 7–10 science class. What would an inclusive response look like?
4.orientationNote: Topic 5 moves from the principles of differentiation explored in Topic 4 into the practical challenge of designing genuinely inclusive Years 7–10 science classrooms. The adjustment strategies, UDL principles, and equity frameworks you encounter this week are directly applicable to your work with diverse 7–10 science cohorts — and they also underpin the assessment design and unit planning you will engage with in Topic 6. As you work through the Topic 5 materials, pay attention to the reasoning behind specific adjustments: understanding why a particular strategy maintains access to the curriculum while preserving the integrity of the learning outcome will prepare you to make similar decisions in your own planning.

5.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 1: The Planning Process

topics.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 1: The Planning Process

announcementBody.intro: This week we bring together your curriculum knowledge, pedagogical understanding, and resource evaluation skills to focus on the discipline of unit planning — one of the most important and complex professional tasks you will undertake as a Years 7–10 science teacher.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 6A examines the planning process: how to establish outcomes, select and sequence content, embed formative assessment, choose resources, and make a constructivist pedagogical approach visible and coherent across a five-week unit. The decisions you practise this week are the same decisions Assessment Task 2 requires you to demonstrate.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 6A is the most directly assessed topic in this unit. Assessment Task 2 requires you to plan a five-week unit program using a named constructivist approach, embed formative and summative assessment, and design a summative task with an A–E rubric — and the unit planning principles you study this week are the same principles the AT2 marking rubric assesses. AT2 is due 6 September. If you have not yet begun drafting your unit outline, this week is the right time to start. Work through Topic 6A with AT2 Part A in mind: use the live session to get early peer feedback on your emerging unit design, and bring questions about constructive alignment and sequencing to the session.

announcementBody.intro: This week we bring together your curriculum knowledge, pedagogical understanding, and resource evaluation skills to focus on the discipline of unit planning — one of the most important and complex professional tasks you will undertake as a Years 7–10 science teacher.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 6A examines the planning process: how to establish outcomes, select and sequence content, embed formative assessment, choose resources, and make a constructivist pedagogical approach visible and coherent across a five-week unit. The decisions you practise this week are the same decisions Assessment Task 2 requires you to demonstrate.

5.liveSessionFocus: Session 5 focuses on Part 1 of unit planning — the planning process. We work through the decisions that shape a coherent, outcomes-aligned unit program: establishing outcomes, sequencing content, selecting resources, and embedding formative assessment.
liveSessionTasks: Select a Knowledge and Understanding strand from the NSW Science Years 7–10 (2026) syllabus and sketch a rough five-week unit outline — identify the key outcomes, main content sequence, and at least two formative assessment opportunities.

Evaluate your outline against the unit planning principles in the topic materials: Is the constructivist approach visible? Is the sequence outcomes-focused? Does it build in opportunities for feedback?
Come prepared to share your outline and receive peer feedback in the session — bring a willingness to revise.
5.orientationNote: Topic 6A is the most directly assessed topic in this unit. Assessment Task 2 requires you to plan a five-week unit program using a named constructivist approach, embed formative and summative assessment, and design a summative task with an A–E rubric — and the unit planning principles you study this week are the same principles the AT2 marking rubric assesses. AT2 is due 6 September. If you have not yet begun drafting your unit outline, this week is the right time to start. Work through Topic 6A with AT2 Part A in mind: use the live session to get early peer feedback on your emerging unit design, and bring questions about constructive alignment and sequencing to the session.

6.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 2: The Role of Assessment and Evaluation

topics.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 2: The Role of Assessment and Evaluation

announcementBody.intro: This week we turn to the assessment and evaluation dimension of unit planning — the professional skill of designing assessment tasks that give all students a genuine opportunity to demonstrate their learning, and that give you reliable evidence to improve your teaching.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 6B examines assessment design, formative and summative assessment practice, and program evaluation. You will work through the principles of constructive alignment, the design of A–E marking rubrics using NESA Performance Band Descriptors, and the role of formative assessment in informing teaching decisions for a diverse cohort. A scaffolded critique activity this week gives you practice at identifying alignment gaps and rubric weaknesses before Assessment Task 2 requires you to design your own.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 6B prepares you directly for Assessment Task 2 Part C, which requires you to design a summative assessment task with an A–E marking rubric aligned to your unit. The critique activity this week is the only scaffolded practice opportunity for this skill before it is assessed — take it seriously. Post to the forum before the live session so you can discuss your analysis with peers. Students who engage critically with the sample task this week consistently produce stronger rubrics in AT2 Part C. AT2 is due 6 September.

6.liveSessionFocus: Session 6 focuses on Part 2 of unit planning — assessment and evaluation. We examine how to design assessment tasks that are aligned to outcomes, inclusive for all learners, and supported by marking rubrics that give students clear, usable guidance.
liveSessionTasks: Before the session, complete the Topic 6B assessment critique activity: read the sample assessment task and post your analysis to the forum, identifying at least one alignment gap, two rubric problems, and one inclusion concern for the given cohort.
Read at least one peer’s forum post and note one point of agreement and one point you would push back on — bring this to the session.
Come prepared to discuss: what makes a summative assessment task genuinely inclusive without lowering the standard of the curriculum?
6.orientationNote: Topic 6B prepares you directly for Assessment Task 2 Part C, which requires you to design a summative assessment task with an A–E marking rubric aligned to your unit. The critique activity this week is the only scaffolded practice opportunity for this skill before it is assessed — take it seriously. Post to the forum before the live session so you can discuss your analysis with peers. Students who engage critically with the sample task this week consistently produce stronger rubrics in AT2 Part C. AT2 is due 6 September.

7.title: Teaching and Learning Science Using Analogies

topics.title: Teaching and Learning Science Using Analogies

announcementBody.intro: This week we examine one of the most powerful — and most frequently misused — pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the analogy. Used well, analogies make abstract science concepts concrete and accessible. Used poorly, they embed misconceptions that are hard to shift.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 7 focuses on analogies as tools for conceptual change in science learning. You will examine the theory behind why analogies work, explore frameworks for selecting and presenting analogies effectively (including the Teaching-With-Analogies model), and critically evaluate common analogies used in Years 7–10 science for their accuracy, accessibility, and potential to mislead.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 7 develops one of the most practically powerful pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the capacity to select, adapt, and critically evaluate analogies and models for specific science concepts. This connects directly to LO1 (Science Content and Laboratory Practice) and LO3 (Lesson Planning and Conceptual Development). A science teacher who understands where an analogy illuminates and where it breaks down is better equipped to address student misconceptions early and to make abstract concepts genuinely accessible for junior secondary learners. As you work through Topic 7, practise applying the Teaching-With-Analogies framework to concepts from your own teaching area — and notice where analogies you have encountered in textbooks or online resources may mislead students.

announcementBody.intro: This week we examine one of the most powerful — and most frequently misused — pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the analogy. Used well, analogies make abstract science concepts concrete and accessible. Used poorly, they embed misconceptions that are hard to shift.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 7 focuses on analogies as tools for conceptual change in science learning. You will examine the theory behind why analogies work, explore frameworks for selecting and presenting analogies effectively (including the Teaching-With-Analogies model), and critically evaluate common analogies used in Years 7–10 science for their accuracy, accessibility, and potential to mislead.

7.liveSessionFocus: Session 7 examines analogies as pedagogical tools in Years 7–10 science — working through examples from across the 7–10 syllabus and practising the skill of evaluating an analogy before using it with students.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one analogy commonly used in your Years 7–10 science teaching area — write down what concept it is meant to explain, what it maps correctly, and where the mapping breaks down.
Consider how you would introduce this analogy to a class while explicitly addressing its limitations, so students build accurate conceptual understanding rather than a flawed mental model.
Come prepared to present your analogy briefly and receive feedback from peers on how they experience it as learners.
7.orientationNote: Topic 7 develops one of the most practically powerful pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the capacity to select, adapt, and critically evaluate analogies and models for specific science concepts. This connects directly to LO1 (Science Content and Laboratory Practice) and LO3 (Lesson Planning and Conceptual Development). A science teacher who understands where an analogy illuminates and where it breaks down is better equipped to address student misconceptions early and to make abstract concepts genuinely accessible for junior secondary learners. As you work through Topic 7, practise applying the Teaching-With-Analogies framework to concepts from your own teaching area — and notice where analogies you have encountered in textbooks or online resources may mislead students.

8.title: Developing Your Science Content Knowledge

topics.title: Developing Your Science Content Knowledge

announcementBody.intro: In our final teaching week, we return to a foundational professional responsibility: the ongoing development of your own science content knowledge. Strong content knowledge is not just a starting point — it is something you actively build and deepen throughout your teaching career.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 8 focuses on content knowledge development as a professional discipline. You will conduct a systematic audit of your content knowledge against your NSW Science Years 7–10 (2026) syllabus, identify specific gaps and priorities, and build a realistic plan for ongoing learning — including professional associations, subject-matter resources, and peer networks that support science content development.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 8 closes the teaching program by turning attention to your own science content knowledge — a professional responsibility that continues throughout your career. This connects directly to LO6 and Assessment Task 2 Part D, due 6 September. AT2 Part D requires you to name a specific external professional or community source that could improve your content knowledge or teaching, and to explain how you would access that support. Students who have genuinely engaged with the professional associations, subject-matter resources, and peer networks Topic 8 points you toward are able to name those sources with specificity and conviction in their reflection — not generically. Use this week's audit activity seriously: the content gaps you identify now are the preparation time you have before AT2 requires you to demonstrate confidence and professional direction.

8.liveSessionFocus: Session 8 covers Topics 7 and 8 together. We examine analogies and content knowledge development as complementary professional skills, and conclude the teaching program by looking ahead to the professional learning habits that will sustain your practice in Years 7–10 science.
liveSessionTasks: Complete a content knowledge audit for your primary Years 7–10 teaching area: rate your confidence across the major content sections and identify your two or three priority gaps.
For each priority gap, identify at least one concrete resource or action you will take to address it — a specific book, online course, professional association, or peer with relevant expertise.
Come prepared to briefly share your audit findings and your plan — and to give and receive constructive feedback with peers.
8.orientationNote: Topic 8 closes the teaching program by turning attention to your own science content knowledge — a professional responsibility that continues throughout your career. This connects directly to LO6 and Assessment Task 2 Part D, due 6 September.
